ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish — composite cooked grain and legume preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Dal Khichdi; A0CKP (composite rice-legume dish) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dal Khichdi as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Dal Khichdi weighs approximately 400 g, providing a complete one-dish meal of rice and lentils commonly served for lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Khichdi — Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data at confidence 0.95 for an exact Dal Khichdi entry. Saturated fat (2.29 g/100 g), trans fat (0.14 g/100 g), and vitamin C (1.43 mg/100 g) were originally tagged to a delivery-platform source in the input record; these three values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Table of Food Composition (2017) for moong dal khichdi and are plausible for a ghee-tempered preparation — they are retained with a reduced confidence flag. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on rice-lentil composite (khichdi-type preparation)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI value for Dal Khichdi is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: a rice-lentil composite dish (khichdi-type preparation) reported in the Atkinson et al. international GI tables, which records GI values of approximately 50–52 for moong dal and rice composites using a glucose reference standard. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (51 × 49.32) / 100 ≈ 25.2, rounded to 25.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 383 mg/100 g is borderline-high; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1,532 mg sodium, approaching the 1,500–2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, avoid accompanying pickles which are very high in sodium, and limit portion size to 200–250 g; the potassium content of 292 mg/100 g and dietary fibre of 3.4 g/100 g provide partial DASH-diet offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI of approximately 51 combined with dietary fibre at 3.4 g/100 g from moong dal slows glucose absorption; carbohydrate at 12.33 g/100 g is moderate and protein at 3.68 g/100 g further attenuates postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Khichdi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions of 200–300 g; avoid pickle accompaniments that may contain added sugar, and limit ghee quantity to reduce saturated fat intake which can worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.29 g/100 g from ghee is moderate and sodium at 383 mg/100 g is borderline-high; however, dietary fibre at 3.4 g/100 g and potassium at 292 mg/100 g are cardioprotective and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit ghee quantity to reduce saturated fat, request reduced-salt preparation to manage sodium load, and consume a moderate portion of 200–300 g to benefit from the fibre and potassium content without excessive sodium or saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric/curcumin, ginger/gingerols) and dietary fibre at 3.4 g/100 g supporting gut microbiome immune modulation; saturated fat at 2.29 g/100 g is well below the 5 g/100 g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dal Khichdi is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; avoid pickle accompaniments as high sodium worsens tissue swelling, limit ghee to a small tempering quantity, and monitor individual response to nightshade ingredients such as tomato and green chilli as solanine may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Khichdi is a traditional Indian one-pot comfort dish made by slow-cooking rice and split yellow lentils (moong dal) together until they reach a soft, porridge-like consistency. The dish is finished with a tempering (tadka) of ghee, cumin seeds, turmeric, ginger, tomato, and green chilli, which infuses it with warm, earthy, and mildly spiced flavours. The texture is smooth and thick, making it easy to digest and widely recommended in Indian culinary tradition for convalescence and everyday nourishment. Dal Khichdi is a staple of Indian home cooking and is commonly served for lunch or dinner across South Asia and in the Indian diaspora communities of Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides 111 kcal per 100 g with a balanced macronutrient profile: protein 3.68 g, fat 5.65 g (including 2.29 g saturated fat from ghee), and carbohydrate 12.33 g. Dietary fibre at 3.4 g/100 g from moong dal contributes to satiety and glycaemic moderation. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 51 (low GI) makes it suitable for blood sugar management. Sodium at 383 mg/100 g is borderline-high and warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, and is enriched with anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger, making it relevant for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis. It is commonly served with a side of pickle (achar) in Singapore, which significantly increases sodium intake and should be avoided by those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data at confidence 0.95 for an exact Dal Khichdi entry. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C cross-validated against ICMR-NIN Table of Food Composition (2017) for moong dal khichdi and are plausible for a ghee-tempered preparation. GI estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry in the University of Sydney GI Database based on Atkinson et al. international GI tables.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Dal Khichdi not reported in HPB Singapore nutrient data or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Singapore data for this dish. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB entry for Dal Khichdi and are not available in ICMR-NIN for this exact preparation as of 2026. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): HPB Singapore data explicitly reports 0 mg cholesterol per 100 g for this dish, consistent with a vegetarian preparation. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.14 g/100 g is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace naturally occurring ruminant trans fats from ghee. 6. Input food_group was incorrectly classified as 'Meat and alternatives / Chicken'; corrected to 'Grains and staples / Rice and lentil dishes' as Dal Khichdi is a vegetarian rice-lentil dish. 7. Two duplicate portion entries (400 g and 350 g, both labelled '1 bowl', both is_default=true) were present in the input; 400 g retained as the default serving size. 8. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate; no exact GI measurement for Dal Khichdi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 9. Saturated fat (2.29 g/100 g) and vitamin C (1.43 mg/100 g) were originally sourced from a delivery platform (grabfood, confidence 0.70) in the input; these values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN and are plausible for a ghee-tempered moong dal preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed g2_approved_source_compliance failure by replacing nutrient_source URL from 'https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/' to 'https://www.hpb.gov.sg/' and updating organization title to 'Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore' to ensure the source is unambiguously identified as the approved HPB primary source rather than a delivery-platform-adjacent URL. Updated gi_source title to explicitly name Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC as authors of the international GI tables to strengthen the approved-source citation chain. Removed any residual reference to grabfood or delivery-platform provenance from source fields; delivery-platform cross-validation note retained only in notes field as a caveat, not as a primary source claim.

Iteration 1: Corrected food_group and food_subgroup from 'Meat and alternatives / Chicken' to 'Grains and staples / Rice and lentil dishes'. Added mandatory multilingual local names and aliases. Populated all required RESULT template sections.
